flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of binary search using recursion?
flashcards.back: O(log n) - The search space is halved with each recursive call, making it highly efficient for large datasets.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of recursive binary search?
flashcards.back: O(log n) - Due to the recursive call stack, which stores log₂(n) levels of recursion.

flashcards.front: What is the prerequisite for using binary search on an array?
flashcards.back: The array MUST be sorted in ascending or descending order. Binary search will not work on unsorted arrays.

flashcards.front: What are the three key parameters in a recursive binary search function?
flashcards.back: 1) The array (arr) 2) The search key (target) 3) The indices (low and high) defining the current search range.

flashcards.front: What is the base case in recursive binary search when element is found?
flashcards.back: When arr[mid] equals the target element, return the index mid as the position of the element.

flashcards.front: What are the two base cases in binary search when element is NOT found?
flashcards.back: 1) low > high (search space exhausted) 2) The array becomes empty with no valid range to search.

flashcards.front: How is the middle index calculated in binary search?
flashcards.back: mid = low + (high - low) / 2. This prevents integer overflow in languages with fixed-size integers.

flashcards.front: In recursive binary search, what happens when target < arr[mid]?
flashcards.back: The target lies in the LEFT half, so we recursively call binarySearch(arr, low, mid-1, target).

flashcards.front: In recursive binary search, what happens when target > arr[mid]?
flashcards.back: The target lies in the RIGHT half, so we recursively call binarySearch(arr, mid+1, high, target).

flashcards.front: Why is binary search preferred over linear search for large sorted arrays?
flashcards.back: Binary search has O(log n) complexity vs linear search O(n), making it exponentially faster for large datasets.

flashcards.front: What is the divide-and-conquer approach in binary search?
flashcards.back: The problem is divided into half at each step (divide), the target is searched in one half (conquer), and solutions are combined implicitly through recursion.

flashcards.front: What value is returned if the target element is not found in binary search?
flashcards.back: Typically returns -1 to indicate unsuccessful search. Some implementations may return a boolean or use an out-parameter.

flashcards.front: How many comparisons maximum are needed to find an element in an array of 100 elements using binary search?
flashcards.back: At most 7 comparisons (log₂100 ≈ 6.64, rounded up to 7).

flashcards.front: What is tail recursion in binary search?
flashcards.back: Binary search is NOT tail-recursive because recursive calls are made in both branches (left and right), not just one. Each recursive call may do additional work after the call returns.

flashcards.front: Why is it important to update mid-1 and mid+1 rather than mid in recursive calls?
flashcards.back: Using mid would cause infinite recursion. mid-1 excludes the already-checked middle element from the left search space; mid+1 excludes it from the right search space.
